value.Title: California investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind project

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH9-GYY3-S9NN-T0V9-00000-00California investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind project2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:59:47ZLexisNexisENVIRONMENTCalifornia investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind projectJENNIFER McDERMOTT

California is investigating one of the Trump administration's deals to end an offshore wind project.

Golden State Wind was a floating offshore wind project proposed off California's central coast. The California Energy Commission said Monday it issued an administrative subpoena to Golden State Wind.

The commission said it is seeking documents and information about the company's recent agreement with the Department of Interior to accept a payout in exchange for voluntarily abandoning its offshore wind lease.

"The Trump administration is recklessly spending billions of taxpayer dollars on backroom deals that would turn back the clock on innovation," CEC Chair David Hochschild said in a statement. "Californians deserve immediate answers about the nature of this payout. Taxpayer dollars should be used to build a sustainable energy future, not to pay to make projects disappear."

The Trump administration is spending nearly $2 billion to get energy companies to walk away from U.S. offshore wind projects. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum said companies were sold a product that was only viable when propped up by massive taxpayer subsidies when they bid for these offshore wind leases in 2022, under former President Joe Biden.

The Republican administration adopted this strategy after federal courts thwarted President Donald Trump's efforts to stop offshore wind development through executive action. Three agreements have been announced.

Under the first deal, made public in March, French company TotalEnergies is getting $1 billion essentially a refund of its leases for offshore wind projects off North Carolina and New York if it invests the money in fossil fuel projects instead. In the latest deals announced last week, the administration said Golden State Wind and Bluepoint Wind agreed to end their leases in exchange for reimbursements totaling nearly $900 million, provided they invest equally in fossil fuels.

Both Golden State and Bluepoint are co-owned by Ocean Winds, a joint venture of EDP Renewables and French energy giant Engie. Bluepoint Wind was an offshore wind project in the early stages of development off the coasts of New Jersey and New York.

When asked about the subpoena Monday, Ocean Winds said it does not comment on open or potential litigation.

This investigation sets the stage for legal action from California to safeguard renewable energy, as well as the thousands of jobs and millions of dollars of investment the state was counting on, said Eddie Ahn, executive director of Brightline Defense, an environmental justice nonprofit working to advance offshore wind in California.

A letter from California Attorney General Rob Bonta's office to Golden State Wind says the state anticipates potential litigation involving the federal government and parties to lease buyouts impacting California's energy needs and offshore wind programs. California has invested about $100 million to support offshore wind development in order to accelerate the state's transition to clean energy and address climate change.

Democrats in Congress are investigating, too. U.S. Reps. Jared Huffman of California, the top Democrat on the House Natural Resources Committee, and Jamie Raskin, the ranking Democrat on the House Judiciary Committee, are demanding information about TotalEnergies agreement.

urn:contentItem:6JHB-B2C3-S487-92VM-00000-00California investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind project2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:59:47ZLexisNexisENVIRONMENTCalifornia investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind projectJENNIFER McDERMOTT

California is investigating one of the Trump administration's deals to end an offshore wind project.

Golden State Wind was a floating offshore wind project proposed off California's central coast. The California Energy Commission said Monday it issued an administrative subpoena to Golden State Wind.

The commission said it is seeking documents and information about the company's recent agreement with the Department of Interior to accept a payout in exchange for voluntarily abandoning its offshore wind lease.

"The Trump administration is recklessly spending billions of taxpayer dollars on backroom deals that would turn back the clock on innovation," CEC Chair David Hochschild said in a statement. "Californians deserve immediate answers about the nature of this payout. Taxpayer dollars should be used to build a sustainable energy future, not to pay to make projects disappear."

The Trump administration is spending nearly $2 billion to get energy companies to walk away from U.S. offshore wind projects. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum said companies were sold a product that was only viable when propped up by massive taxpayer subsidies when they bid for these offshore wind leases in 2022, under former President Joe Biden.

The Republican administration adopted this strategy after federal courts thwarted President Donald Trump's efforts to stop offshore wind development through executive action. Three agreements have been announced.

Under the first deal, made public in March, French company TotalEnergies is getting $1 billion essentially a refund of its leases for offshore wind projects off North Carolina and New York if it invests the money in fossil fuel projects instead. In the latest deals announced last week, the administration said Golden State Wind and Bluepoint Wind agreed to end their leases in exchange for reimbursements totaling nearly $900 million, provided they invest equally in fossil fuels.

Both Golden State and Bluepoint are co-owned by Ocean Winds, a joint venture of EDP Renewables and French energy giant Engie. Bluepoint Wind was an offshore wind project in the early stages of development off the coasts of New Jersey and New York.

When asked about the subpoena Monday, Ocean Winds said it does not comment on open or potential litigation.

This investigation sets the stage for legal action from California to safeguard renewable energy, as well as the thousands of jobs and millions of dollars of investment the state was counting on, said Eddie Ahn, executive director of Brightline Defense, an environmental justice nonprofit working to advance offshore wind in California.

A letter from California Attorney General Rob Bonta's office to Golden State Wind says the state anticipates potential litigation involving the federal government and parties to lease buyouts impacting California's energy needs and offshore wind programs. California has invested about $100 million to support offshore wind development in order to accelerate the state's transition to clean energy and address climate change.

Democrats in Congress are investigating, too. U.S. Reps. Jared Huffman of California, the top Democrat on the House Natural Resources Committee, and Jamie Raskin, the ranking Democrat on the House Judiciary Committee, are demanding information about TotalEnergies agreement.

urn:contentItem:6JH9-GYY3-S9NN-T120-00000-00California investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind project2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:59:47ZLexisNexisENVIRONMENTCalifornia investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind projectJENNIFER McDERMOTT

California is investigating one of the Trump administration's deals to end an offshore wind project.

Golden State Wind was a floating offshore wind project proposed off California's central coast. The California Energy Commission said Monday it issued an administrative subpoena to Golden State Wind.

The commission said it is seeking documents and information about the company's recent agreement with the Department of Interior to accept a payout in exchange for voluntarily abandoning its offshore wind lease.

"The Trump administration is recklessly spending billions of taxpayer dollars on backroom deals that would turn back the clock on innovation," CEC Chair David Hochschild said in a statement. "Californians deserve immediate answers about the nature of this payout. Taxpayer dollars should be used to build a sustainable energy future, not to pay to make projects disappear."

The Trump administration is spending nearly $2 billion to get energy companies to walk away from U.S. offshore wind projects. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum said companies were sold a product that was only viable when propped up by massive taxpayer subsidies when they bid for these offshore wind leases in 2022, under former President Joe Biden.

The Republican administration adopted this strategy after federal courts thwarted President Donald Trump's efforts to stop offshore wind development through executive action. Three agreements have been announced.

Under the first deal, made public in March, French company TotalEnergies is getting $1 billion essentially a refund of its leases for offshore wind projects off North Carolina and New York if it invests the money in fossil fuel projects instead. In the latest deals announced last week, the administration said Golden State Wind and Bluepoint Wind agreed to end their leases in exchange for reimbursements totaling nearly $900 million, provided they invest equally in fossil fuels.

Both Golden State and Bluepoint are co-owned by Ocean Winds, a joint venture of EDP Renewables and French energy giant Engie. Bluepoint Wind was an offshore wind project in the early stages of development off the coasts of New Jersey and New York.

When asked about the subpoena Monday, Ocean Winds said it does not comment on open or potential litigation.

This investigation sets the stage for legal action from California to safeguard renewable energy, as well as the thousands of jobs and millions of dollars of investment the state was counting on, said Eddie Ahn, executive director of Brightline Defense, an environmental justice nonprofit working to advance offshore wind in California.

A letter from California Attorney General Rob Bonta's office to Golden State Wind says the state anticipates potential litigation involving the federal government and parties to lease buyouts impacting California's energy needs and offshore wind programs. California has invested about $100 million to support offshore wind development in order to accelerate the state's transition to clean energy and address climate change.

Democrats in Congress are investigating, too. U.S. Reps. Jared Huffman of California, the top Democrat on the House Natural Resources Committee, and Jamie Raskin, the ranking Democrat on the House Judiciary Committee, are demanding information about TotalEnergies agreement.

value.Title: California investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind project
value.Overview: The Oakland Press (Pontiac, Michigan), NATIONAL POLITICS, 599words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH9-GYY3-S9NN-T0H8-00000-00California investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind project2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:59:47ZLexisNexisNATIONAL POLITICSCalifornia investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind projectJENNIFER McDERMOTT

California is investigating one of the Trump administration's deals to end an offshore wind project.

Golden State Wind was a floating offshore wind project proposed off California's central coast. The California Energy Commission said Monday it issued an administrative subpoena to Golden State Wind.

The commission said it is seeking documents and information about the company's recent agreement with the Department of Interior to accept a payout in exchange for voluntarily abandoning its offshore wind lease.

"The Trump administration is recklessly spending billions of taxpayer dollars on backroom deals that would turn back the clock on innovation," CEC Chair David Hochschild said in a statement. "Californians deserve immediate answers about the nature of this payout. Taxpayer dollars should be used to build a sustainable energy future, not to pay to make projects disappear."

The Trump administration is spending nearly $2 billion to get energy companies to walk away from U.S. offshore wind projects. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum said companies were sold a product that was only viable when propped up by massive taxpayer subsidies when they bid for these offshore wind leases in 2022, under former President Joe Biden.

The Republican administration adopted this strategy after federal courts thwarted President Donald Trump's efforts to stop offshore wind development through executive action. Three agreements have been announced.

Under the first deal, made public in March, French company TotalEnergies is getting $1 billion essentially a refund of its leases for offshore wind projects off North Carolina and New York if it invests the money in fossil fuel projects instead. In the latest deals announced last week, the administration said Golden State Wind and Bluepoint Wind agreed to end their leases in exchange for reimbursements totaling nearly $900 million, provided they invest equally in fossil fuels.

Both Golden State and Bluepoint are co-owned by Ocean Winds, a joint venture of EDP Renewables and French energy giant Engie. Bluepoint Wind was an offshore wind project in the early stages of development off the coasts of New Jersey and New York.

When asked about the subpoena Monday, Ocean Winds said it does not comment on open or potential litigation.

This investigation sets the stage for legal action from California to safeguard renewable energy, as well as the thousands of jobs and millions of dollars of investment the state was counting on, said Eddie Ahn, executive director of Brightline Defense, an environmental justice nonprofit working to advance offshore wind in California.

A letter from California Attorney General Rob Bonta's office to Golden State Wind says the state anticipates potential litigation involving the federal government and parties to lease buyouts impacting California's energy needs and offshore wind programs. California has invested about $100 million to support offshore wind development in order to accelerate the state's transition to clean energy and address climate change.

Democrats in Congress are investigating, too. U.S. Reps. Jared Huffman of California, the top Democrat on the House Natural Resources Committee, and Jamie Raskin, the ranking Democrat on the House Judiciary Committee, are demanding information about TotalEnergies agreement.

The Associated Press' climate and environmental coverage receives financial support from multiple private foundations. AP is solely responsible for all content. Find AP's standards for working with philanthropies, a list of supporters and funded coverage areas at AP.org.

urn:contentItem:6JGY-VSX3-S55N-04W5-00000-00Dominion Energy's dividend in perspective: comparison with DTE Energy Company *Updated*2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:07:21ZLexisNexisDominion Energy's dividend in perspective: comparison with DTE Energy Company *Updated*

NYSE STOCK REPORT

Dominion Energy's trailing twelve months dividend is $2.67. Its dividend yield at its current price is 4.14%. Its current price is at a 3.5% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 28.5% premium to its 52 week low. If Dominion Energy was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 5.32%.

In comparison, DTE Energy Company's trailing twelve months dividend is $4.51. Its dividend yield at its current price is 2.97%. Its current price is at a 1.1% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 21.1% premium to its 52 week low. If DTE Energy Company was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 3.6%.

$1000 invested into Dominion Energy stock 5 years ago would be worth $1,243 today. This total gain of $243 is generated by a capital gain of $18 and dividends of $224.

$1000 invested into DTE Energy Company stock 5 years ago would be worth $1,679 today. This total gain of $679 is generated by a capital gain of $456 and dividends of $223.

Dominion Energy's dividend has grown 0% over the last 4 years.

DTE Energy Company's dividend has grown 7.1% over the last 4 years.

Company Profile

Dominion Energy operates primarily in regulated electricity and natural gas services, with its largest revenue sources stemming from electric operations in Virginia, North Carolina, and South Carolina, serving 3.6 million customers. Natural gas distribution in South Carolina, with 500,000 customers, also contributes significantly to revenue. In 2025, the company generated $16.5 billion in operating revenue, primarily from these segments. The fastest-growing sections include contracted energy, which saw a 22% year-over-year increase in operating earnings to $438 million, driven by investments in renewable energy projects such as offshore wind and solar. Additionally, Dominion is the largest producer of carbon-free electricity in New England, leveraging nuclear production to support its clean energy initiatives.

value.Title: ACT: CLEAN ENERGY LOWERS YOUR BILLS

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHD-GDV3-S5K0-Y28V-00000-00ACT: CLEAN ENERGY LOWERS YOUR BILLS2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:07:21ZLexisNexisACT: CLEAN ENERGY LOWERS YOUR BILLSStates News ServiceSAN FRANCISCO, CA

The following information was released by the Sierra Club:

Friend,

Across the country, families are feeling the strain of rising energy, fuel, and utility bills -- and are being forced to make tough choices. At the same time, energy demand is climbing fast due to the prolific growth of data centers and the digital economy. There is a clear, proven path forward with clean energy, but it's being deliberately blocked.

Renewable energy like wind and solar is already lowering costs, creating jobs, and delivering clean, reliable power to millions of homes.

That's why it's so alarming that the Trump administration is cutting backroom deals with fossil fuel billionaires instead of investing in solutions that will help lower our energy bills. In March, officials announced a nearly $1 billion agreement with TotalEnergies to cancel offshore wind projects off the coasts of North Carolina and New York -- projects that would have delivered affordable, clean power -- in exchange for expanding methane gas exports in Texas. And just this week, two more buy-outs were announced - deals that lock us into higher bills and more pollution, while putting taxpayers on the hook.

Trump is setting yet another dangerous precedent. If more companies walk away from their clean energy projects, it will stall progress, kill jobs, and drive energy prices even higher for families like yours -- especially as the growth of data centers drives more energy demand. These aren't just policy decisions -- they are choices that take affordable, reliable energy off the table for millions of families.

But here's the truth: clean energy is still winning.

Offshore wind projects are already delivering reliable power to the grid, proving that solutions are available now. In courtrooms across the country, judges are pushing back on unlawful attacks against clean energy, allowing projects to move forward and workers to get back on the job. Now, it's time for Congress to step in.

Call on Congress to investigate these backroom deals, protect wind and solar projects, and stand up for people forced to choose between keeping the lights on and putting food on the table.

We can't afford to let political deals drive up our energy bills. When we speak out, we can stop these backroom deals that benefit billionaires at our expense and build a future powered by clean energy that works for everyone.

In Solidarity,

Sierra Club

value.Title: California investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind project
value.Overview: Pasadena Star-News: Web Edition Articles (California), ENVIRONMENT, 599words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHB-1D73-S5JS-C027-00000-00California investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind project2026-05-28T15:59:47Z2026-05-28T15:59:47ZLexisNexisENVIRONMENTCalifornia investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind projectJENNIFER McDERMOTT

California is investigating one of the Trump administration's deals to end an offshore wind project.

Golden State Wind was a floating offshore wind project proposed off California's central coast. The California Energy Commission said Monday it issued an administrative subpoena to Golden State Wind.

The commission said it is seeking documents and information about the company's recent agreement with the Department of Interior to accept a payout in exchange for voluntarily abandoning its offshore wind lease.

"The Trump administration is recklessly spending billions of taxpayer dollars on backroom deals that would turn back the clock on innovation," CEC Chair David Hochschild said in a statement. "Californians deserve immediate answers about the nature of this payout. Taxpayer dollars should be used to build a sustainable energy future, not to pay to make projects disappear."

The Trump administration is spending nearly $2 billion to get energy companies to walk away from U.S. offshore wind projects. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum said companies were sold a product that was only viable when propped up by massive taxpayer subsidies when they bid for these offshore wind leases in 2022, under former President Joe Biden.

The Republican administration adopted this strategy after federal courts thwarted President Donald Trump's efforts to stop offshore wind development through executive action. Three agreements have been announced.

Under the first deal, made public in March, French company TotalEnergies is getting $1 billion essentially a refund of its leases for offshore wind projects off North Carolina and New York if it invests the money in fossil fuel projects instead. In the latest deals announced last week, the administration said Golden State Wind and Bluepoint Wind agreed to end their leases in exchange for reimbursements totaling nearly $900 million, provided they invest equally in fossil fuels.

Both Golden State and Bluepoint are co-owned by Ocean Winds, a joint venture of EDP Renewables and French energy giant Engie. Bluepoint Wind was an offshore wind project in the early stages of development off the coasts of New Jersey and New York.

When asked about the subpoena Monday, Ocean Winds said it does not comment on open or potential litigation.

This investigation sets the stage for legal action from California to safeguard renewable energy, as well as the thousands of jobs and millions of dollars of investment the state was counting on, said Eddie Ahn, executive director of Brightline Defense, an environmental justice nonprofit working to advance offshore wind in California.

A letter from California Attorney General Rob Bonta's office to Golden State Wind says the state anticipates potential litigation involving the federal government and parties to lease buyouts impacting California's energy needs and offshore wind programs. California has invested about $100 million to support offshore wind development in order to accelerate the state's transition to clean energy and address climate change.

Democrats in Congress are investigating, too. U.S. Reps. Jared Huffman of California, the top Democrat on the House Natural Resources Committee, and Jamie Raskin, the ranking Democrat on the House Judiciary Committee, are demanding information about TotalEnergies agreement.

NYSE STOCK REPORT

Dominion Energy's trailing twelve months dividend is $2.67. Its dividend yield at its current price is 4.14%. Its current price is at a 3.5% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 28.5% premium to its 52 week low. If Dominion Energy was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 5.32%.

In comparison, WEC Energy Group, Inc's trailing twelve months dividend is $3.63. Its dividend yield at its current price is 3.08%. Its current price is at a 1.4% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 20.1% premium to its 52 week low. If WEC Energy Group, Inc was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 3.7%.

$1000 invested into Dominion Energy stock 5 years ago would be worth $1,243 today. This total gain of $243 is generated by a capital gain of $18 and dividends of $224.

$1000 invested into WEC Energy Group, Inc stock 5 years ago would be worth $1,688 today. This total gain of $688 is generated by a capital gain of $457 and dividends of $231.

Dominion Energy's dividend has grown 0% over the last 4 years.

WEC Energy Group, Inc's dividend has grown 7% over the last 4 years.

Company Profile

Dominion Energy operates primarily in regulated electricity and natural gas services, with its largest revenue sources stemming from electric operations in Virginia, North Carolina, and South Carolina, serving 3.6 million customers. Natural gas distribution in South Carolina, with 500,000 customers, also contributes significantly to revenue. In 2025, the company generated $16.5 billion in operating revenue, primarily from these segments. The fastest-growing sections include contracted energy, which saw a 22% year-over-year increase in operating earnings to $438 million, driven by investments in renewable energy projects such as offshore wind and solar. Additionally, Dominion is the largest producer of carbon-free electricity in New England, leveraging nuclear production to support its clean energy initiatives.

value.Jurisdiction: North Carolina
value.Title: Climate activists block governor's mansion, call Stein 'complicit' with Duke Energy
value.Overview: The Herald Sun (Durham, N.C.), CLIMATE CHANGE NEWS, 511words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHD-YW33-RS13-J2KB-00000-00Climate activists block governor's mansion, call Stein 'complicit' with Duke Energy2026-05-28T16:07:21Z2026-05-28T16:07:21ZLexisNexisCLIMATE CHANGE NEWSClimate activists block governor's mansion, call Stein 'complicit' with Duke EnergyJosh Shaffer

Climate activists blocked the entrance to the Governor's Mansion Wednesday, calling for an end to Duke Energy's fossil-fuel expansion and accusing Gov. Josh Stein of being "complicit" in policies that intensify hurricanes, wildfires and drought.

By 3 p.m., six activists associated with Durham-based nonprofit NC Warn had occupied the Blount Street gate for three hours, resisting three requests to move from Capitol police. They later moved to a lower and more heavily trafficked gate on Jones Street.

No arrests have yet been made.

"We are here in good conscience," said Jim Warren, executive director of NC Warn. "We ask that the government tell the truth about Duke Energy as a climate polluter. ... Instead, he keeps repeating the corporate propaganda."

NC Warn pointed to Duke Energy's planned 12,000 megawatt fossil fuel expansion as part of a 50-year strategy to ignore climate change data - including its own research - while touting plans to become carbon-neutral by 2050.

Warren said the 2050 plan is "bogus" and irrelevant considering the damage North Carolina will suffer in the next two decades.

Protesters also pointed to Duke calling for a rate hike while earning nearly $5 billion in 2025.

Stein has opposed this hike and accused Duke of backing away from clean energy policies.

"Duke Energy's proposed rate hike is simply too high and comes as the company is also retreating on more affordable clean energy," Stein said in December. "At a time when families are struggling to make ends meet, we should be doing everything we can to make life more affordable, not less. I will continue to fight on behalf of every North Carolinian to lower costs and grow the economy."

Asked Wednesday, a spokesperson said the Governor has vetoed the General Assembly's bill that would increase utility bills, shift costs of industrial users to residential ratepayers and slow efforts to bring down emissions.

"After lawmakers overrode his veto," the spokesperson said, "Governor Stein supported Attorney General Jackson's efforts to challenge Duke Energy's excessive rate hikes and called on the Utilities Commission to require significant changes to Duke Energy's Carolinas Resource Plan, which retreated on affordable clean energy. The state needs an energy system that protects consumers, reduces pollution, and supports economic growth."

Warren on Wednesday called the governor's effort insufficient and said Duke is "forcing us to pay for our own demise."

More than 60 scientists wrote an open letter to Stein in April warning of "climate catastrophe," urging him to speak out on Duke's reliance on fossil fuels and reluctance to transition to renewable energy.

At a rally before the six protesters blocked the gate, roughly 20 more joined the chorus for louder environmental truth.

"As the hurricane season rapidly approaches, the stress of living in what has become Hurricane Alley haunts us," said Bobby Jones, an environmental justice advocate from Goldsboro. "We should no longer be forced by Duke Energy to finance our own demise."

Duke Energy did not issue a response.

value.Title: Earth Month 2026: How Everpure Employees Took Action Around the World

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJX-RNC3-RW43-7198-00000-00Earth Month 2026: How Everpure Employees Took Action Around the World2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:58ZLexisNexisEarth Month 2026: How Everpure Employees Took Action Around the World

Last month, Everpure employees throughout the world learned, listened, and volunteered in support of the environment.

NORTHAMPTON, MA / ACCESS Newswire / May 13, 2026 / During Earth Month 2026, Everpure deepened its commitment to sustainability as employees worldwide volunteered in initiatives focused on climate action, renewable energy education, ecosystem restoration, and circular economy projects.

In April, Everpure employees around the world came together to turn our sustainability values into action for Earth Month. In collaboration with the Everpure Foundation, teams hosted hands-on volunteer events, learning sessions, and local upcycling activities that made environmental stewardship tangible in offices and communities alike. Over 10% of our global employees participated in Earth Month activities, demonstrating our commitment to sustainability and a culture of purpose.

Employees in Singapore created energy light stations for local students to teach youth about solar power.

Renewable energy and climate education

Employees came together to provide tools for sustainable energy and empower the next generation with environmental knowledge.

Solar energy stations: Employees in Singapore and Raleigh-Durham, North Carolina, built stations to teach children about the mechanics and benefits of renewable energy for local nonprofit organizations Kampung Senang and El Futuro North Carolina.

Solar lamp construction: Teams in Lehi, Utah, assembled solar lamps to help Community Action Services & Food Bank promote the practical use of solar energy.

Climate action kits: In India, teammates created educational kits designed to engage local youth in climate advocacy, benefiting Bangalore-based Need Base India.

Virtual design-thinking challenge: Across the US, employees participated in a virtual hackathon hosted by Team4Tech, brainstorming solutions to support climate advocate user communities and stakeholder engagement.

Sustainability panel: Employees globally tuned in to hear from representatives throughout the company on how they're embedding sustainability into their corporate strategies.

Biodiversity and ecosystem restoration

This theme covered "boots-on-the-ground" work to repair natural habitats and support local wildlife populations.

Reforestation: In Prague, employees collaborated with longtime partner Sázíme Stromy, planting trees to restore biodiversity on deforested land.

Seed ball creation: In Calgary, employees and customers worked together to create seed balls for CPAWS Southern Alberta, a nonprofit conservation organization working to promote thriving, healthy, and diverse lands and waters in Alberta.

Wildlife habitat protection: The Santa Clara team collaborated with South Bay Clean Creeks Coalition to remove trash and debris from Los Gatos Creek, specifically to support the health of local salmon and beaver populations.

Marine research: Employees throughout Europe analyzed images of the Great Barrier Reef, assisting Citizens of the Reef in their quest to deliver broad-scale data to reef managers globally.

Community cleanups: Employees in Dublin, Ireland; Lehi, Utah; and Staines, UK, rolled up their sleeves to clean up trash from their local parks.

Employees in Dublin, Ireland, clean up trash at their local park.

Circular economy and digital footprint

In addition to volunteering, employees focused on reducing waste and making conscious shifts in daily habits.

Upcycling and composting: Employees in Prague and Santa Clara gave new life to old materials by upcycling clothing and backpacks.

E-waste and digital trash cleanup: Team members around the world dropped off old electronics for recycling and reduced their environmental footprint by taking out digital trash.

Sustainability panel: Subject matter experts from engineering, finance, supply chain, and more came together to discuss how they're embedding sustainability into their business strategies.

Employees in Prague upcycled used clothing with our new brand.

Earth Month celebrations culminated in a fireside chat with professional climber and climate advocate Alex Honnold, who shared his journey and how the Honnold Foundation is using community-scale solar projects to advance climate resilience and energy equity around the world. Employees rallied around these efforts, raising $20,000 for the Honnold Foundation's programs.

These efforts are part of a broader Everpure commitment to embed sustainability into how we work every day-from our people to our facilities to our products. By combining local volunteerism, listening sessions, and hands-on workshops, Everpure and the Everpure Foundation are helping employees turn climate concerns into concrete steps for people and the planet. As we close out Earth Month, one thing is clear: When our global community leans in together, small actions add up to meaningful, measurable impact.

Find more stories and multimedia from Everpure at 3blmedia.com.

value.Title: Guest Editorial Trump’s wind energy grudge hits California

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJF-TS63-S8MW-F4S2-00000-00Guest Editorial2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:07:21ZLexisNexisFEATURESPg. 11Guest EditorialTrump’s wind energy grudge hits California

The Trump administration has hit a new low in its crusade against renewable energy. It will pay two wind farm developers hundreds of millions of dollars to walk away from viable clean energy projects. One of them, Golden State Wind’s 2-gigawatt project off Morro Bay, could have powered 1.1 million California homes.

The Interior Department will pay $885 million to the two companies. Making matters worse, the companies must spend their windfalls on what the department calls “reliable conventional energy.” That is code for oil and gas.

This follows a similar deal in March paying $928 million to a French-owned company to abandon leases off New York and North Carolina.

These payoffs are the president’s personal pique posing as public policy.

President Donald Trump’s hostility toward wind power goes back at least 20 years. In 2006, he bought a stretch of Scottish coastline for a golf resort and soon learned that the Scots planned an offshore wind farm that would sit in the course’s sea views. He complained, “I want to see the ocean, I do not want to see windmills.”

Trump sued. He lost, appealed and lost again all the way up to the U.K. Supreme Court. The turbines went up.

His antipathy toward wind power continues in the White House.

He tried to halt all wind permitting with an executive order issued on his first day back in office. A federal judge struck that down.

Now he throws money at it, preferably other people’s, to get what he wants. In addition to the payoffs for canceled leases, his administration withdrew hundreds of millions of dollars meant to fund offshore wind projects, including $427 million pledged to Humboldt Bay.

Rep. Jared Huffman, D-San Rafael, has rightly called the latest deal “outrageous” and “almost certainly unlawful.” The problem is that it is far from clear that anyone has legal standing to file a lawsuit against the payoffs. Huffman instead hopes that the Government Accountability Office will investigate whether the administration tapped improper funds for the payoffs.

Legalities and Trump’s personal disdain for wind power aside, these moves will make it all the harder for the nation and California to meet rising energy demands, let alone meet them responsibly.

The California Energy Commission predicts that peak electricity demand will increase by 42% to 61% by 2045. Data centers, electric vehicles and building electrification are driving that increase.

But where will that electricity come from without aggressively deploying every clean source available, including wind power?

And that’s just baseline growth in demand. If Californians hope to retire Diablo Canyon, the state’s last nuclear plant, and phase out in-state oil production, the state will need even more power to replace those lost sources.

An all-of-the-above clean energy strategy is essential. Solar, battery storage, wind on land and sea, and geothermal power all should be part of the mix. Indeed, geothermal might be California’s quiet advantage, and the North Bay is an epicenter for it.

The Geysers, which sits on the border of Sonoma and Lake counties, is the world’s largest geothermal complex. Constellation Energy’s $26.6 billion acquisition Geyers company Calpine brings deep-pocketed new ownership to the field. Meanwhile, Sonoma County won $1.36 million in state funding to map the best sites for next-generation geothermal, and Sonoma Clean Power’s GeoZone initiative seeks to add 600 megawatts to the 725 megawatts already produced there.

California must continue to build for its future energy needs, even if the president pays energy developers to walk away.

Written by the Press Democrat editorial board. ©2025 The Press Democrat.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHM-T3H3-S7MW-X3MP-00000-00Pilot Travel Centers LLC Trademark Application for "PILOT" Filed2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:07:21ZLexisNexisPILOT TRAVEL CENTERS LLCPilot Travel Centers LLC Trademark Application for "PILOT" Filed


2026 MAY 07 (NewsRx) -- By a News Reporter-Staff News Editor at Ecology Daily News -- According to news reporting originating from Washington, D.C., by NewsRx journalists, a trademark application has been made for "PILOT" by Seth L. Hudson Maynard Nexsen PC, representing Pilot Travel Centers LLC. This application was made available to the public on April 24, 2026.


The serial number for this application is 97776750.
The international trademark goods and services class codes for this trademark application are 001, 003, 004, 009, 029, 030, 035, 037 and 039.
As submitted by the applicant, this trademark application relates to the following goods and services:
• Charging stations for electric vehicles; downloadable mobile application featuring software for tracking electricity prices for charging electric vehicles, tracking locations of charging stations for electric vehicles, tracking availability of charging stations for electric vehicles, and facilitating secure payment transactions for electric vehicle charging;
• Diesel exhaust fluid; chemical additives for fuel treatment; antifreeze; coolants; transmission fluid;
• Natural gas; compressed natural gas; renewable natural gas; electrical energy; gaseous and liquid hydrogen fuel; engine oil;
• Vehicle battery charging; recharging services for electric vehicles, namely, charging of electric vehicles; fueling of hydrogen gas for vehicles; natural gas refueling service for motor vehicles;
• Prepared Entrees consisting primarily of cooked chicken; garden salads, fruit salads, soups, chili, cooked chicken, hash brown potatoes, chicken tenders, hot dogs, hard boiled eggs, fried potato wedges, baked beans, cooked green beans, country fried steaks of meat, mashed potatoes, processed corn in the nature of cooked corn, chicken wings, and meatloaf; packaged meals consisting primarily of meat, poultry, or vegetables;
• Windshield washing fluid;
• Retail store services featuring renewable natural gas, compressed natural gas, gaseous and liquid hydrogen fuel, and electrical energy; energy management services, namely, providing a service that allows customers to purchase energy, namely, electricity, natural gas, gaseous and liquid hydrogen fuel, and renewable energy at a fixed price;
• Distribution of electricity; transportation of renewable natural gas and compressed natural gas and gaseous and liquid hydrogen fuel for others; distribution of renewable natural gas and compressed natural gas and gaseous and liquid hydrogen fuel; renewable natural gas supplying; compressed natural gas supplying;
• Sandwiches, wrap sandwiches, coffee, biscuits, tea, pizza, oatmeal, hamburger sandwiches, macaroni and cheese, bagel sandwiches, croissant sandwiches, cheeseburger sandwiches, cocoa, cappuccino, coffee-based beverages, namely, lattes; packaged meals consisting primarily of pasta and rice.
The registrar information for this application is: Seth L. HudsonMaynardNexsen PC, 227 WEST TRADE STREET, SUITE 2300, CHARLOTTE, NC 28202, United States.
Keywords for this news article include: Business, Elements, Energy, Gases, Hydrogen, Inorganic Chemicals, Natural Gas, Oil & Gas, Pilot Travel Centers LLC, Renewable Energy.

value.Title: California investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind project
value.Overview: Associated Press International, DOMESTIC NEWS; STATE AND REGIONAL, 598words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH3-R3B3-S2JP-D1PD-00000-00California investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind project2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:59:47ZLexisNexisDOMESTIC NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALCalifornia investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind projectJENNIFER McDERMOTTAssociated Press

California is investigating one of the Trump administration's deals to end an offshore wind project.

Golden State Wind was a floating offshore wind project proposed off California’s central coast. The California Energy Commission said Monday it issued an administrative subpoena to Golden State Wind.

The commission said it is seeking documents and information about the company's recent agreement with the Department of Interior to accept a payout in exchange for voluntarily abandoning its offshore wind lease.

“The Trump administration is recklessly spending billions of taxpayer dollars on backroom deals that would turn back the clock on innovation,” CEC Chair David Hochschild said in a statement. “Californians deserve immediate answers about the nature of this payout. Taxpayer dollars should be used to build a sustainable energy future, not to pay to make projects disappear.”

The Trump administration is spending nearly $2 billion to get energy companies to walk away from U.S. offshore wind projects. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum said companies were sold a product that was only viable when propped up by massive taxpayer subsidies when they bid for these offshore wind leases in 2022, under former President Joe Biden.

The Republican administration adopted this strategy after federal courts thwarted President Donald Trump’s efforts to stop offshore wind development through executive action. Three agreements have been announced.

Under the first deal, made public in March, French company TotalEnergies is getting $1 billion — essentially a refund of its leases for offshore wind projects off North Carolina and New York — if it invests the money in fossil fuel projects instead. In the latest deals announced last week, the administration said Golden State Wind and Bluepoint Wind agreed to end their leases in exchange for reimbursements totaling nearly $900 million, provided they invest equally in fossil fuels.

Both Golden State and Bluepoint are co-owned by Ocean Winds, a joint venture of EDP Renewables and French energy giant Engie. Bluepoint Wind was an offshore wind project in the early stages of development off the coasts of New Jersey and New York.

When asked about the subpoena Monday, Ocean Winds said it does not comment on open or potential litigation.

This investigation sets the stage for legal action from California to safeguard renewable energy, as well as the thousands of jobs and millions of dollars of investment the state was counting on, said Eddie Ahn, executive director of Brightline Defense, an environmental justice nonprofit working to advance offshore wind in California.

A letter from California Attorney General Rob Bonta’s office to Golden State Wind says the state anticipates potential litigation involving the federal government and parties to lease buyouts impacting California’s energy needs and offshore wind programs. California has invested about $100 million to support offshore wind development in order to accelerate the state's transition to clean energy and address climate change.

Democrats in Congress are investigating, too. U.S. Reps. Jared Huffman of California, the top Democrat on the House Natural Resources Committee, and Jamie Raskin, the ranking Democrat on the House Judiciary Committee, are demanding information about TotalEnergies agreement.

___

urn:contentItem:6JHD-YW33-RS13-J2JC-00000-00Climate activists block governor's mansion, call Stein 'complicit' with Duke Energy2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:07:21ZLexisNexisCLIMATE CHANGE NEWSClimate activists block governor's mansion, call Stein 'complicit' with Duke EnergyJosh Shaffer

Climate activists blocked the entrance to the Governor's Mansion Wednesday, calling for an end to Duke Energy's fossil-fuel expansion and accusing Gov. Josh Stein of being "complicit" in policies that intensify hurricanes, wildfires and drought.

By 3 p.m., six activists associated with Durham-based nonprofit NC Warn had occupied the Blount Street gate for three hours, resisting three requests to move from Capitol police. They later moved to a lower and more heavily trafficked gate on Jones Street.

No arrests have yet been made.

"We are here in good conscience," said Jim Warren, executive director of NC Warn. "We ask that the government tell the truth about Duke Energy as a climate polluter. ... Instead, he keeps repeating the corporate propaganda."

NC Warn pointed to Duke Energy's planned 12,000 megawatt fossil fuel expansion as part of a 50-year strategy to ignore climate change data - including its own research - while touting plans to become carbon-neutral by 2050.

Warren said the 2050 plan is "bogus" and irrelevant considering the damage North Carolina will suffer in the next two decades.

Protesters also pointed to Duke calling for a rate hike while earning nearly $5 billion in 2025.

Stein has opposed this hike and accused Duke of backing away from clean energy policies.

"Duke Energy's proposed rate hike is simply too high and comes as the company is also retreating on more affordable clean energy," Stein said in December. "At a time when families are struggling to make ends meet, we should be doing everything we can to make life more affordable, not less. I will continue to fight on behalf of every North Carolinian to lower costs and grow the economy."

Asked Wednesday, a spokesperson said the Governor has vetoed the General Assembly's bill that would increase utility bills, shift costs of industrial users to residential ratepayers and slow efforts to bring down emissions.

"After lawmakers overrode his veto," the spokesperson said, "Governor Stein supported Attorney General Jackson's efforts to challenge Duke Energy's excessive rate hikes and called on the Utilities Commission to require significant changes to Duke Energy's Carolinas Resource Plan, which retreated on affordable clean energy. The state needs an energy system that protects consumers, reduces pollution, and supports economic growth."

Warren on Wednesday called the governor's effort insufficient and said Duke is "forcing us to pay for our own demise."

More than 60 scientists wrote an open letter to Stein in April warning of "climate catastrophe," urging him to speak out on Duke's reliance on fossil fuels and reluctance to transition to renewable energy.

At a rally before the six protesters blocked the gate, roughly 20 more joined the chorus for louder environmental truth.

"As the hurricane season rapidly approaches, the stress of living in what has become Hurricane Alley haunts us," said Bobby Jones, an environmental justice advocate from Goldsboro. "We should no longer be forced by Duke Energy to finance our own demise."

Duke Energy did not issue a response.

urn:contentItem:6JHD-YW33-RS13-J2KR-00000-00Climate activists block governor's mansion, call Stein 'complicit' with Duke Energy2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:07:21ZLexisNexisCLIMATE CHANGE NEWSClimate activists block governor's mansion, call Stein 'complicit' with Duke EnergyJosh Shaffer

Climate activists blocked the entrance to the Governor's Mansion Wednesday, calling for an end to Duke Energy's fossil-fuel expansion and accusing Gov. Josh Stein of being "complicit" in policies that intensify hurricanes, wildfires and drought.

By 3 p.m., six activists associated with Durham-based nonprofit NC Warn had occupied the Blount Street gate for three hours, resisting three requests to move from Capitol police. They later moved to a lower and more heavily trafficked gate on Jones Street.

No arrests have yet been made.

"We are here in good conscience," said Jim Warren, executive director of NC Warn. "We ask that the government tell the truth about Duke Energy as a climate polluter. ... Instead, he keeps repeating the corporate propaganda."

NC Warn pointed to Duke Energy's planned 12,000 megawatt fossil fuel expansion as part of a 50-year strategy to ignore climate change data - including its own research - while touting plans to become carbon-neutral by 2050.

Warren said the 2050 plan is "bogus" and irrelevant considering the damage North Carolina will suffer in the next two decades.

Protesters also pointed to Duke calling for a rate hike while earning nearly $5 billion in 2025.

Stein has opposed this hike and accused Duke of backing away from clean energy policies.

"Duke Energy's proposed rate hike is simply too high and comes as the company is also retreating on more affordable clean energy," Stein said in December. "At a time when families are struggling to make ends meet, we should be doing everything we can to make life more affordable, not less. I will continue to fight on behalf of every North Carolinian to lower costs and grow the economy."

Asked Wednesday, a spokesperson said the Governor has vetoed the General Assembly's bill that would increase utility bills, shift costs of industrial users to residential ratepayers and slow efforts to bring down emissions.

"After lawmakers overrode his veto," the spokesperson said, "Governor Stein supported Attorney General Jackson's efforts to challenge Duke Energy's excessive rate hikes and called on the Utilities Commission to require significant changes to Duke Energy's Carolinas Resource Plan, which retreated on affordable clean energy. The state needs an energy system that protects consumers, reduces pollution, and supports economic growth."

Warren on Wednesday called the governor's effort insufficient and said Duke is "forcing us to pay for our own demise."

More than 60 scientists wrote an open letter to Stein in April warning of "climate catastrophe," urging him to speak out on Duke's reliance on fossil fuels and reluctance to transition to renewable energy.

At a rally before the six protesters blocked the gate, roughly 20 more joined the chorus for louder environmental truth.

"As the hurricane season rapidly approaches, the stress of living in what has become Hurricane Alley haunts us," said Bobby Jones, an environmental justice advocate from Goldsboro. "We should no longer be forced by Duke Energy to finance our own demise."

Duke Energy did not issue a response.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH3-PYS3-RV1V-B182-00000-00California investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind project2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:59:47ZLexisNexisDOMESTIC NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALCalifornia investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind projectJENNIFER McDERMOTTAssociated Press

California is investigating one of the Trump administration's deals to end an offshore wind project.

Golden State Wind was a floating offshore wind project proposed off California’s central coast. The California Energy Commission said Monday it issued an administrative subpoena to Golden State Wind.

The commission said it is seeking documents and information about the company's recent agreement with the Department of Interior to accept a payout in exchange for voluntarily abandoning its offshore wind lease.

“The Trump administration is recklessly spending billions of taxpayer dollars on backroom deals that would turn back the clock on innovation,” CEC Chair David Hochschild said in a statement. “Californians deserve immediate answers about the nature of this payout. Taxpayer dollars should be used to build a sustainable energy future, not to pay to make projects disappear.”

The Trump administration is spending nearly $2 billion to get energy companies to walk away from U.S. offshore wind projects. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum said companies were sold a product that was only viable when propped up by massive taxpayer subsidies when they bid for these offshore wind leases in 2022, under former President Joe Biden.

The Republican administration adopted this strategy after federal courts thwarted President Donald Trump’s efforts to stop offshore wind development through executive action. Three agreements have been announced.

Under the first deal, made public in March, French company TotalEnergies is getting $1 billion — essentially a refund of its leases for offshore wind projects off North Carolina and New York — if it invests the money in fossil fuel projects instead. In the latest deals announced last week, the administration said Golden State Wind and Bluepoint Wind agreed to end their leases in exchange for reimbursements totaling nearly $900 million, provided they invest equally in fossil fuels.

Both Golden State and Bluepoint are co-owned by Ocean Winds, a joint venture of EDP Renewables and French energy giant Engie. Bluepoint Wind was an offshore wind project in the early stages of development off the coasts of New Jersey and New York.

When asked about the subpoena Monday, Ocean Winds said it does not comment on open or potential litigation.

This investigation sets the stage for legal action from California to safeguard renewable energy, as well as the thousands of jobs and millions of dollars of investment the state was counting on, said Eddie Ahn, executive director of Brightline Defense, an environmental justice nonprofit working to advance offshore wind in California.

A letter from California Attorney General Rob Bonta’s office to Golden State Wind says the state anticipates potential litigation involving the federal government and parties to lease buyouts impacting California’s energy needs and offshore wind programs. California has invested about $100 million to support offshore wind development in order to accelerate the state's transition to clean energy and address climate change.

Democrats in Congress are investigating, too. U.S. Reps. Jared Huffman of California, the top Democrat on the House Natural Resources Committee, and Jamie Raskin, the ranking Democrat on the House Judiciary Committee, are demanding information about TotalEnergies agreement.

urn:contentItem:6JH3-R3B3-S1TT-23H8-00000-00California investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind project2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:59:47ZLexisNexisDOMESTIC NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALCalifornia investigates Trump administration's deal to end an offshore wind projectJENNIFER McDERMOTTAssociated Press

California is investigating one of the Trump administration's deals to end an offshore wind project.

Golden State Wind was a floating offshore wind project proposed off California’s central coast. The California Energy Commission said Monday it issued an administrative subpoena to Golden State Wind.

The commission said it is seeking documents and information about the company's recent agreement with the Department of Interior to accept a payout in exchange for voluntarily abandoning its offshore wind lease.

“The Trump administration is recklessly spending billions of taxpayer dollars on backroom deals that would turn back the clock on innovation,” CEC Chair David Hochschild said in a statement. “Californians deserve immediate answers about the nature of this payout. Taxpayer dollars should be used to build a sustainable energy future, not to pay to make projects disappear.”

The Trump administration is spending nearly $2 billion to get energy companies to walk away from U.S. offshore wind projects. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum said companies were sold a product that was only viable when propped up by massive taxpayer subsidies when they bid for these offshore wind leases in 2022, under former President Joe Biden.

The Republican administration adopted this strategy after federal courts thwarted President Donald Trump’s efforts to stop offshore wind development through executive action. Three agreements have been announced.

Under the first deal, made public in March, French company TotalEnergies is getting $1 billion — essentially a refund of its leases for offshore wind projects off North Carolina and New York — if it invests the money in fossil fuel projects instead. In the latest deals announced last week, the administration said Golden State Wind and Bluepoint Wind agreed to end their leases in exchange for reimbursements totaling nearly $900 million, provided they invest equally in fossil fuels.

Both Golden State and Bluepoint are co-owned by Ocean Winds, a joint venture of EDP Renewables and French energy giant Engie. Bluepoint Wind was an offshore wind project in the early stages of development off the coasts of New Jersey and New York.

When asked about the subpoena Monday, Ocean Winds said it does not comment on open or potential litigation.

This investigation sets the stage for legal action from California to safeguard renewable energy, as well as the thousands of jobs and millions of dollars of investment the state was counting on, said Eddie Ahn, executive director of Brightline Defense, an environmental justice nonprofit working to advance offshore wind in California.

A letter from California Attorney General Rob Bonta’s office to Golden State Wind says the state anticipates potential litigation involving the federal government and parties to lease buyouts impacting California’s energy needs and offshore wind programs. California has invested about $100 million to support offshore wind development in order to accelerate the state's transition to clean energy and address climate change.

Democrats in Congress are investigating, too. U.S. Reps. Jared Huffman of California, the top Democrat on the House Natural Resources Committee, and Jamie Raskin, the ranking Democrat on the House Judiciary Committee, are demanding information about TotalEnergies agreement.

___

urn:contentItem:6JG9-M1R3-RT73-61FJ-00000-00Siemens Announces €40 Million Lithium Venture as Part of Major Restructuring2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:07:21ZLexisNexisSiemens Announces €40 Million Lithium Venture as Part of Major Restructuring

DAILY ADR AND DEPOSITORY RECEIPT REPORT

UNITED STATES EDITION

01 May 2026 06:15 EDT

Siemens shares gained approximately 1.5 percent to €250.35 on Wednesday as the industrial powerhouse revealed a series of strategic operational developments, including a significant lithium partnership in Europe and the inauguration of a new manufacturing plant in the United States. These announcements come just weeks ahead of the company’s second-quarter earnings report, scheduled for 13th May 2026, as investors grapple with a sweeping internal reorganization that could redefine Siemens' corporate structure.

The most notable development is a framework agreement with Vulcan Energy for the 'Lionheart' lithium project situated in the Upper Rhine Graben. Siemens is set to provide a comprehensive array of automation, engineering, and telecommunications solutions for both the extraction facility in Landau and the processing plant in Frankfurt-H chst. The initial order volume is pegged at €40 million, but the partnership is positioned for longevity, with Siemens Financial Services committing €67 million toward the total project cost of €2.2 billion. This initiative aims to produce lithium hydroxide from geothermal brine while concurrently generating renewable energy, a move that aligns with Europe's increasing demand for battery raw materials.

In parallel, Siemens Mobility has successfully completed its $220 million manufacturing facility in Lexington, North Carolina. The plant commenced operations in April 2026, with the first 25 passenger railcars already rolling off the production line. This facility is expected to create approximately 500 jobs by 2028, reinforcing Siemens’ commitment to bolstering its manufacturing capabilities in the U.S. rail sector.

Behind the scenes, Siemens CEO Roland Busch is spearheading a potentially transformative internal restructuring. Reports suggest that Siemens plans to dissolve its two core divisions, Digital Industries and Smart Infrastructure, in favor of establishing six or seven smaller units. This initiative aims to eliminate silos and integrate infrastructure, transport, software, and artificial intelligence under a consolidated 'One Tech Company' strategy. The mobility division remains unaffected by this restructuring, with discussions between the supervisory board and worker representatives anticipated in May 2026.

Analyst sentiment appears to be shifting in favor of Siemens, with HSBC upgrading the company from 'Hold' to 'Buy' in late April and raising its price target from €240 to €300. Bernstein Research has noted that Siemens' ongoing €6 billion share buyback program is nearing completion and anticipates a follow-up program of at least €10 billion. As Siemens prepares for its earnings release on 13th May, investors will be keenly watching for insights on margin trends and the overall impact of tariff pressures on the company’s full-year guidance.

IN TODAY'S REPORT:

SECTION 1 SIEMENS ACTIVITIES

SECTION 1 Siemens Activities

Activities

Siemens AG, headquartered in Munich, Germany, is a global leader in technology and engineering, specializing in electrification, automation, and digitalization. The company's core business activities span diverse sectors, including energy solutions, healthcare systems, industrial software, mobility infrastructure, and smart building technologies. Siemens delivers innovative products and services that enhance efficiency, sustainability, and connectivity across industries. Among its fastest-growing segments are digital industries, driven by demand for AI-powered automation and IoT solutions in manufacturing; sustainable energy initiatives, such as wind power and grid management systems; and healthcare advancements, including advanced imaging and diagnostic tools. This strategic focus on high-growth areas enables Siemens to maintain a competitive edge, fostering long-term value through technological innovation and global partnerships. It is the International Depository Receipt (IDR) market's largest Miscellaneous manufacturing company by market capitalisation.

value.Title: Week in review Some iPhone owners could get up to $95 from settlement

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ7-VMP3-RTCD-P0Y9-00000-00Week in review2026-05-10T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:07:21ZLexisNexisBUSINESSPg. 11Week in reviewSome iPhone owners could get up to $95 from settlement

Owners of some iPhones are in line to get cash payments of up to $95 from Apple after the company on Tuesday reached a $250 million settlement in a class-action lawsuit for false advertising of its artificial intelligence capabilities.

Apple trumpeted new AI features for its virtual assistant Siri when it rolled out the iPhone 16 in 2024, part of new software updates that the company billed as “Apple Intelligence.”

The company has been scrambling to keep up with tech rivals amid the AI boom but still hasn’t delivered on the Siri revamp two years later.

The lawsuit, filed on behalf of U.S. consumers in the San Franciscofederal court for the Northern District of California, alleged that Apple deceived consumers with a marketing campaign that promoted features that did not yet exist and misled them into buying the devices.

Lawyers for the iPhone buyers asked a court for preliminary approval of the proposed $250 million settlement, according to a court filling. If approved by a judge, it would be one of the biggest ever for Apple.

The settlement covers about 37 million devices bought in the U.S. from June 10, 2024, to March 29, 2025, including all iPhone 16 models and the iPhone 15 Pro and iPhone 15 Pro Max.

State probing Trump deal on wind project

California is investigating one of the Donald Trump administration’s deals to end an offshore wind project.

Golden State Wind was a floating offshore wind project proposed off California’s central coast. The California Energy Commission said Monday it issued an administrative subpoena to Golden State Wind.

The commission said it is seeking documents and information about the company’s recent agreement with the Department of Interior to accept a payout in exchange for voluntarily abandoning its offshore wind lease.

The Trump administration is spending nearly $2 billion to get energy companies to walk away from U.S. offshore wind projects. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum said companies were sold a product that was only viable when propped up by massive taxpayer subsidies when they bid for these offshore wind leases in 2022, under former President Joe Biden.

Under the first deal, French company TotalEnergies is getting $1 billion essentially a refund of its leases for offshore wind projects off North Carolina and New York if it invests the money in fossil fuel projects instead. In the latest deals announced last week, the administration said Golden State Wind and Bluepoint Wind agreed to end their leases in exchange for reimbursements totaling nearly $900 million, provided they invest equally in fossil fuels.

Both Golden State and Bluepoint are co-owned by Ocean Winds, a joint venture of EDP Renewables and French energy giant Engie.

This investigation sets the stage for legal action from California to safeguard renewable energy, as well as the thousands of jobs and millions of dollars of investment the state was counting on, said Eddie Ahn, executive director of Brightline Defense, an environmental justice nonprofit working to advance offshore wind in California.

Fed sees risks rising from inflation

Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis President Alberto Musalem said there is much uncertainty over the future path for the economy and monetary policy, but he sees risks rising more for inflation than for employment.

“Inflation is running meaningfully above our target of 2%,” Musalem said Wednesday during an event in Fairhope, Alabama, organized by the Mississippi Bankers Association. “We have risks both on the employment side and on the inflation side. In my understanding, the risks have been shifting towards more risk on the inflation side than the employment side.”

Musalem said the Fed’s benchmark policy rate is either at the neutral level that neither boosts nor slows the economy, but it might also be slightly accommodative.

“There are very plausible scenarios under which the economy would require us to keep the policy rate at its current level for some time,” he said.

He also noted he sees scenarios under which officials may need to lower rates further or raise rates.

“So a lot of uncertainty right now, and it’s important to see how things settle,” Musalem said.

Compiled from Associated Press and Bloomberg reports.

value.Title: May 11, 2026: - WOMBLE BOND DICKINSON PEER LANDSCAPE: RECENT NEWS HIGHLIGHTS

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJM-G8H3-S3TD-11PB-00000-00May 11, 2026: - WOMBLE BOND DICKINSON PEER LANDSCAPE: RECENT NEWS HIGHLIGHTS2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:07:21ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 11, 2026: - WOMBLE BOND DICKINSON PEER LANDSCAPE: RECENT NEWS HIGHLIGHTS

On April 2, 2026, a legal team from Womble Bond Dickinson, led by attorney Matthew Tilley, successfully represented the North Carolina General Assembly in a landmark ruling from the North Carolina Supreme Court concerning educational inequities. The Supreme Court concluded that trial court judges had overstepped their jurisdiction by mandating the State to implement a comprehensive, multi-billion-dollar plan for educational reform. Chief Justice Paul Newby emphasized the limits of judicial authority in the ruling, stating, "Judges are not experts on education policy. We cannot account for the various policy alternatives or public opinion."

In another significant development on April 2, 2026, Womble Bond Dickinson supported Aeromed Group in completing a complex series of simultaneous strategic acquisitions and financing arrangements. The transaction enhances Aeromed Group's capabilities in advanced materials and international logistics, allowing the company to serve customers in over 70 countries and adding more than 150 employees to its global workforce.

Shifting to peer activities, Reed Smith LLP announced the addition of a new private equity partner to its Los Angeles office, effective May 4, 2026. This appointment brings extensive experience in private equity transactions and merges into Reed Smith's broader strategy to expand its capabilities in a region that has seen substantial capital inflows.

Meanwhile, Jones Day bolstered its litigation team on April 10, 2026, by welcoming Paul Stancil as Of Counsel in its Business & Tort Litigation Practice in Dallas. Additionally, on April 1, 2026, the firm announced the hiring of four former U.S. Supreme Court clerks as associates to expand its issues and appeals practice.

In the biopharmaceutical sector, Sidley Austin advised Telix Pharmaceuticals on a strategic collaboration with Regeneron Pharmaceuticals, announced on April 21, 2026. The agreement, valued at up to $2.1 billion, involves a 50:50 cost and profit-sharing model to co-develop next-generation radiopharmaceutical therapies, with an upfront payment of $40 million for four initial programs. Joshua Hofheimer, a partner at Sidley Austin, noted the complexity of the deal, stating, "These considerations affect governance, regulatory matters, and intellectual property, in particular." Sidley Austin also separately reported a 33% surge in revenue from its City operations as of April 14, 2026.

Turning to corporate acquisitions, Greenberg Traurig, LLP represented Blake L. Sartini on April 30, 2026, in the transition of Golden Entertainment to a private, family-owned operation. Shareholders received a cash dividend of $2.75 and 0.902 shares of VICI Properties Inc. for each share of Golden stock. Co-Managing Shareholder Michael J. Bonner stated, "We were honored to represent Blake L. Sartini in returning Golden Entertainment to private family ownership. We congratulate Blake and the entire Golden Entertainment team!"

In renewable energy developments, Baker McKenzie advised Virgin Media O2 on a long-term Power Purchase Agreement with egg Power on May 5, 2026. The 10-year agreement will deliver solar energy from a Suffolk farm expected to commence operations in 2027. Partner James Wyatt commented, "This agreement represents a further step in Virgin Media O2's transition towards sustainable energy procurement. We were pleased to support our client on another strategically important PPA, reflecting our continued focus on advising on complex energy and decarbonisation driven projects."

Similarly focused on sustainable infrastructure, Norton Rose Fulbright, alongside Latham & Watkins, advised on a $2.2 billion joint venture between Abu Dhabi Future Energy Company PJSC (Masdar) and TotalEnergies, announced on April 21, 2026. The venture will consolidate onshore renewable activities across nine countries in Asia, projecting 3GW of operational capacity and an additional 6GW of assets to become operational by 2030.

Finally, Morgan Lewis & Bockius LLP reported on April 26, 2026, that the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission is easing restrictions on foreign ownership, control, or domination of nuclear facilities. The new regulations, taking effect on July 7, 2026, grant exemptions to countries that are members of the OECD and the Republic of India, shifting away from strict limitations maintained since the mid-twentieth century. In a separate financial milestone, Lime Rock New Energy closed its second fund with $640 million in commitments on April 22, 2026, surpassing its initial $500 million target under the legal guidance of Morgan Lewis.

urn:contentItem:6JJ7-VMP3-RTCD-P090-00000-00Week in review2026-05-10T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:07:21ZLexisNexisBUSINESSPg. 11Week in reviewSome iPhone owners could get up to $95 from settlement

Owners of some iPhones are in line to get cash payments of up to $95 from Apple after the company on Tuesday reached a $250 million settlement in a class-action lawsuit for false advertising of its artificial intelligence capabilities.

Apple trumpeted new AI features for its virtual assistant Siri when it rolled out the iPhone 16 in 2024, part of new software updates that the company billed as “Apple Intelligence.”

The company has been scrambling to keep up with tech rivals amid the AI boom but still hasn’t delivered on the Siri revamp two years later.

The lawsuit, filed on behalf of U.S. consumers in the San Franciscofederal court for the Northern District of California, alleged that Apple deceived consumers with a marketing campaign that promoted features that did not yet exist and misled them into buying the devices.

Lawyers for the iPhone buyers asked a court for preliminary approval of the proposed $250 million settlement, according to a court filling. If approved by a judge, it would be one of the biggest ever for Apple.

The settlement covers about 37 million devices bought in the U.S. from June 10, 2024, to March 29, 2025, including all iPhone 16 models and the iPhone 15 Pro and iPhone 15 Pro Max.

State probing Trump deal on wind project

California is investigating one of the Donald Trump administration’s deals to end an offshore wind project.

Golden State Wind was a floating offshore wind project proposed off California’s central coast. The California Energy Commission said Monday it issued an administrative subpoena to Golden State Wind.

The commission said it is seeking documents and information about the company’s recent agreement with the Department of Interior to accept a payout in exchange for voluntarily abandoning its offshore wind lease.

The Trump administration is spending nearly $2 billion to get energy companies to walk away from U.S. offshore wind projects. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum said companies were sold a product that was only viable when propped up by massive taxpayer subsidies when they bid for these offshore wind leases in 2022, under former President Joe Biden.

Under the first deal, French company TotalEnergies is getting $1 billion essentially a refund of its leases for offshore wind projects off North Carolina and New York if it invests the money in fossil fuel projects instead. In the latest deals announced last week, the administration said Golden State Wind and Bluepoint Wind agreed to end their leases in exchange for reimbursements totaling nearly $900 million, provided they invest equally in fossil fuels.

Both Golden State and Bluepoint are co-owned by Ocean Winds, a joint venture of EDP Renewables and French energy giant Engie.

This investigation sets the stage for legal action from California to safeguard renewable energy, as well as the thousands of jobs and millions of dollars of investment the state was counting on, said Eddie Ahn, executive director of Brightline Defense, an environmental justice nonprofit working to advance offshore wind in California.

Fed sees risks rising from inflation

Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis President Alberto Musalem said there is much uncertainty over the future path for the economy and monetary policy, but he sees risks rising more for inflation than for employment.

“Inflation is running meaningfully above our target of 2%,” Musalem said Wednesday during an event in Fairhope, Alabama, organized by the Mississippi Bankers Association. “We have risks both on the employment side and on the inflation side. In my understanding, the risks have been shifting towards more risk on the inflation side than the employment side.”

Musalem said the Fed’s benchmark policy rate is either at the neutral level that neither boosts nor slows the economy, but it might also be slightly accommodative.

“There are very plausible scenarios under which the economy would require us to keep the policy rate at its current level for some time,” he said.

He also noted he sees scenarios under which officials may need to lower rates further or raise rates.

“So a lot of uncertainty right now, and it’s important to see how things settle,” Musalem said.

Compiled from Associated Press and Bloomberg reports.
